posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
Hello.

I’ve searched a bit around here on WB to find some info on the domaine.
As far as I could understand from @Robert_Panzer, then there was a shift in the winemaking with the 2016 vintage. But how different are the pre and post 2016 wines? How is the oak regime at the domaine?

Any additional info/opinions would be much appreciated.

posts.cooked: Paging @Peter_Chiu!
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
I can’t directly answer your question, but these two 2017s I thought were pretty awesome.

  • 2017 Domaine Michel Mallard & Fils Ladoix 1er Cru La Corvée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Ladoix 1er Cru (7/29/2023)
    This pours light ruby in the glass with touches of purple and pink toward the rim. The nose is immediately expressive and complex, only gaining with air. Aromas waft cinnamon, cherry, plum, woodsy undergrowth, bay leaf, sassafras, dry dusty soil, a touch of blood orange, and a top note of ginger. The palate enters on red plum and cherry turning to medium minus powdery tannin with medium plus blood orange tinged acid, and a mineral, ginger, and cola/sassafras finish. While there is some apparent new oak here, it's quite immaculately integrated. To the point where it's difficult to discern what is driving the spice notes, it seems to be some clear combination of oak, stems, or simply the terrior. Impressive that it can so seamlessly blend all three and overall quite nice stuff. While I often prefer more pure red fruit than this apparent mix of red and darker fruit, this wine does it quite well and I have to say I love it. (93 points)
  • 2017 Domaine Michel Mallard & Fils Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru Les Serpentières - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru (11/29/2022)
    This pours light ruby in the glass with flecks of pink and purple an a light watery rim. The nose is very aromatic and complex showing rose petals grapefruit rind, red cherry, cherry blossom, a whiff of high quality new oak, cinnamon, cardamom, dry stony soil, and a hint of the petrichor I expect will come with bottle age. The palate is wonderfully elegant showing bright, light and airy red fruit on entry turning to silky medium minus tannin and medium plus red fruit and grapefruit tinged acid. The finish is long recalling the rose petal, grapefruit and lightly spiced elements of the nose. This is my first encounter with a wine from Michel Mallard. I've seen the producer recommended online and was curious to try. I have to say I'm blown away with what this is delivering. On day one this did show notable new oak, but it was clearly high quality, well managed, and already integrating. By day two it's barely noticeable. The finesse and complexity on display here is nothing short of stunning. I predict with decades of age ahead this wine will be putting much more expensive wines from more heralded terrior to shame. (94 points)

I thought they were very classically styled, built on elegance. I recently had a 2002 which I also loved, but there certainly was more heft to it. Darker color, perhaps a touch of brett, but I didn’t get any obtrusive oak. I think it went beyond simply the warmer year, but also to a style that while equally classic, is a tad more based on ripeness and extract.

  • 2002 Domaine Michel Mallard & Fils Aloxe-Corton 1er Cru Les Valozières - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Aloxe-Corton 1er Cru (12/5/2023)
    This pours light garnet in the glass with moderate bricking. The nose is just gorgeous from the first pour. Wide open, complex and seemingly at the beginning of a beautiful drinking window showing smoked tea, forest undergrowth, decaying rose petals, smouldering twigs, dried cherry, iodine, dried strawberry, matsutake mushroom, bergamont tea, musky animal fur, spice box, olive brine, and a touch of horse saddle. The complexity here is incredibly impressive and expansive. The palate enters on deep cherry and is still fairly structured with medium dusty, almost powdery tannin and medium acid. The finish replays much of the nose yet isn't quite as broad, seemingly a touch clipped by the structure, but that is picking a nit. All in all this wine was way beyond expectations. It's the kind of wine that could seriously surprise in blind tastings, and fit right in with much more highly regarded producers and terriors. Personally if tasted blind I'd be guessing a very good Morey 1er, though the iodine, brine and horse saddle character combined with some elevated ripeness could have me considering a cooler vintage of a CdP like Pegau. (94 points)

As you may know Michel Mallard is the winemaker at d’Eugenie in Vosne, which is what became of the Rene Engel estate. I think d’Eugenie gets a bad rap for not living up to the former Engel glory and while I haven’t had Eugenie’s earliest wine which might have been where the poor reputation came from, the wines I’ve had from 2010 - 2016 I thought were quite nice.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Thank for the alert - Phil.

I am a follower of Michel Mallard as his wines were available in Quebec, Canada for many years.

He caught my attention more since 2006 as he was hired as -maker the winemaker at d’Eugenie in Vosne ( owned by Chateau Latour ). CdV 2007 - d’Eugenied was named as the wine of the year for France. I

I thought for a while …Eugenie would be the next Bizot…

BUT I guessed wrong.

Mallard wines were bottled in more classic bottles ( for very reasonable priced ) and they were released at least 2 years late.

I love both his red and whites ( so far only 3 were released by SAQ). .

Sadly only SAQ gets his 3 to 5 reds each year and number of the bottles avaible are very smal - fir example his Renard was released by Lottery - limited to 2 botttles per custom.

Corton Renardes or Le Rognet;
Le Toppe au Vert
La Corvee
Les Serpentieresge
CdNuits Village.

J. Morris loved his 2016 reds : page 436 - 2nd Edition : Inside Burgundy.

I bought 101 bottles of CdNuits-Village 2016,. It has been singing for a while.

:

reply_to_user.name: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
Peter brought 2 bottles of his Corton Renardes to a lunch we had in Montreal:

2010 Domaine Michel Mallard & Fils Corton-Les Renardes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Corton Grand Cru (5/14/2022)

May 2022 - Lunch with Peter & friends (Tian Fu): The nose seemed a little tired, showing a good dose of beef broth. It’s this nose that led me to believe this was the oldest of the two. But the first sip is awesome: a great balance of red berries, subdued spices, some stemminess and nice meaty umami notes. Very nice! My favorite of the two but I would drink this right now whereas the 2005 has yet to show its form.

2005 Domaine Michel Mallard & Fils Corton-Les Renardes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Corton Grand Cru (5/14/2022)

May 2022 - Lunch with Peter & friends (Tian Fu): I now see CT notes saying this is over the hill… well not today it ain’t! Deep red showing no bricking. The nose is very expressive with red and black berries, some soft spices, briar and hints of red meat. The palate hasn’t come together yet and there is plenty of structure there. It just feels like this should go another 10 years before it reaches its peak. I guessed this was the younger vintage of the two. What do I know…
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
If you have a chance to get his G-cru, grabes them. I was only allowed to buy 2 bottles of his Redendes 2917 when it was released ( by lottery limited to 2 bottles per customer ). Many of my friend did have a change to buy.

I do not have much experience with his A/Corton 1er crus. For example his Le Toppe au Vert is around CA $130 ( whereas his g-cru is around CD $180). That being said : I always by 2 to 3 bottles each year, They are resting and waiting in my cellars ( to gain bottle-ages ) while I am sipping his Ladoix -1er cru…

I am also in love his lower end Ladoix 1er and SLB 1er as they are around CA $70 to $80,

HIs CdN village at CA $50 to $55 is my favourite entry level reds,

Chateau Latour knows what they are doing; and J. Morris knows what his talking.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
HI Lassek ; here is the info re oak regime of the Domaine : :

J. Morris wrote in his !st Edition : Inside BURGUNDY. - that the father more or lees 100% new oak, his son rowing BACK.

In the 2rd Edition, Michel - Junior - is now in full charge.Imposed some radical changes beginning with moving back to hand-harvest. His whites are picked ripe…

The reds since 2011 are 50% whole clustered and 2016 with no sulfur, 40 to 80 new oak and a tendency to leave higher levels of CO2.Partly in the knowledge that the wines are not released early.

;

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Greg - just my personal observation and comment which I believe it is sincere and valid.

d"s Eugenie was created ( or bought by Chat.Latour) in the year 2006, CdV’s parcel - own by Chat. Latour in Bordeaux) - is one of few best portions on top.

Michel Mallard was lucky ( or his skill in crafting wines was recognized ) and has been put there in full control including finance, I guessed. His first full year was vintage 2007.

The CdV was named as the wine of the year for 2007 in the whole Kingdom of France…

It is extremely rare such a thing could happened in France,.

Similar to a chef who is just put in charge of a new Restaurateur and he was award 3 Michelin Star in his First year… Not the Second year but First.

Think of ( similar to ) the new restaurant in new Cheval -Blanc Hotel ( owned by LV ) in Paris recently.

Meadows finally stated that Chat.Lotour’s CdV ( in Burgundy) is the top wine in the portfolio.

Wine=Seacher shows CdV is cheaper then Ech and G-Ech; and surprising it is everywhere and just priced a couple of hundred dollar more than the Vosne,.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
For those who are interested.

I have not been up-date myself for a while. Curious …so I just checked Inside Burgundy 2nd Edition - J. Morris reported that Chat.Latour also owned 0.04 ha Montrachet and 0.04 ha of B-Montrachet. They are scarcely released commercially - think of B-M of DRC.

The rich gets…

I could understand that chardonnay - which is one of the very neutral grapes - will be more pure without the oak influence.

I had dinner twice when PYCM was visiting his then importer in Montreal, Quebec, Canada.

I asked him if the following rumor is true or false : his whites are so pure and cleanr, because he decorated his house and his cellar all in white.

You know what he said ?

He said nothing, just a small smile.

Then when our host served the Wellington-beef.

He did not take any beef just the dressing.
